retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Treatment / Management -- When do we restore?
retrieved_docs.content: In 2016, The International Caries Consensus Collaboration [2] presented recommendations for carious tissue removal and cavitated lesions management. They based the decision-making process on whether the lesion can be reached by cleaning implements - cleansable lesion - or not - non-cleansable lesion; if surface cavitation is present, the lesion should be considered non-cleansable and active. [2] The resulting consensus was as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A carious tissue consists of four different zones histologically, among which three zones are visible clinically. The outer layer consists of the necrotic zone and contaminated zone containing microbial biofilm, which can be appreciated clinically as soft mineralized tissue of the tooth. This necrotic zone has a very high microbial load. The next zone is the zone of demineralization, characterized by very few microorganisms, minimal nutrients, and an anaerobic atmosphere. This zone can be correlated clinically as leathery dentine. Finally, the innermost zone near the pulp is the translucent zone of a firm, softer dentine. Demineralization and the absence of microorganisms characterize this zone because microbial flora cannot penetrate to this depth.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Treatment / Management -- Non-invasive Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: We do not need to place a filling in a lesion that can be accessed by easy cleaning measures that will disturb the biofilm, such as fluoride-containing toothpaste. [1] The following lesions can be managed conservatively without the need for a filling: White spot lesions, including initial occlusal lesions. [1] [19] When on a bitewing x-ray, approximal lesions are limited to the enamel or are just into dentin. (It is improbable that these lesions are cavitated in modern populations). [1] Cavitated and non-cavitated root surface lesions can be reached by cleaning implements. [20] [1] If a recurrent lesion adjacent to a restoration is cleansable, it does not require restoration. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Periapical abscess: a history of trauma, tooth wear, fracture, caries, or deep restoration may indicate pulpal damage. Vitality testing with electric or thermal stimulation will either provide a negative or inconclusive response. Radiographically, a periapical radiolucency may be evident if the patient is experiencing an acute exacerbation of a chronic periapical lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Apical periodontitis Periapical abscess Periapical granuloma Periapical cyst Cellulitis Abscess Periostitis Osteomyelitis

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The early stages of dental caries and their management frequently pose a dilemma. Dental caries occurs when the biofilm microbiota that normally resides in the oral cavity in homeostasis changes to an acidogenic, aciduric, and cariogenic population due to the frequent consumption of sugars. [2] The result of this shift can be clinically invisible or lead to a net mineral loss within the tooth's hard structures, resulting in a visible carious lesion - a white spot. [1] If plaque control measures are performed, the lesion can regress without the need for invasive intervention. Existing caries should be initially managed by non-invasive procedures (eg, remineralization, biofilm removal, sealing) instead of removing dental tissue. Cavitated lesions should be arrested or controlled following a minimally invasive approach, e.g., repairing a defective restoration instead of replacing it. [2] Dentists should undertake a conservative approach when treating dental caries, and professionals from other dental specialties should detect early carious lesions and make appropriate referrals promptly to improve patients' outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries prognosis depends on the patient's health, maintenance of oral hygiene, and the extent and severity of the lesion. Ideally, full recovery of an initial caries lesion would be achieved with the described non-operative measures and are preferred. Despite this, a minimal intervention therapy can also be used, like pit and fissure sealants using resin-based or glass ionomer agents). Dentists should only consider restoring if the initial lesion progresses. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Active lesion: The enamel surface is whitish/yellowish, opaque, and typically located close to the gingival margin. It is commonly covered with plaque, with no clinically detectable loss of substance. It feels rough to the touch when the curved part of the explorer is moved softly among the surface - using a sharp explorer to evaluate the lesion's surface is contraindicated because it can break down the enamel. [10] Inactive lesion: The enamel surface is darker and shiny but intact. It feels smooth and hard to the touch; despite that, it is not recommended to evaluate these lesions with an explorer. [11] Furthermore, as part of the evaluation, patients are classified into two groups considering their risk to develop caries:

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: At first, the maxillary incisors present a white demineralization band along the gingival margin, usually missed by parents. The demineralization bands gradually progress to cavities that encircle the teeth' necks in a brown-black collar. By contrast, the lower incisors are unaffected - either completely healthy or mildly carious. Severe cases may show the crowns of the upper incisors destroyed, leaving root stumps.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Existing caries should be initially managed by non-invasive procedures (e.g., remineralization, biofilm removal, sealing) instead of removing dental tissue. Cavitated lesions should be arrested or controlled following a minimally invasive approach, e.g., repairing a defective restoration instead of replacing it. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: We can classify non-cavitated caries lesions as active and inactive, each with different characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of initial caries lesions by demineralization is a significant advance in the clinical management of dental caries, where the main goal is to preserve tooth structure and arrest or reverse the process to prevent the lesion from cavitating. The dentist must acquire a more conservative look, motivating their patients to practice prevention: regularly visiting the dental office, brushing teeth at least twice a day with fluoride toothpaste, adequate exposure to fluorides, and a diet low in fermentable carbohydrates. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Issues of Concern -- Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment for initial caries lesion (even when active) is managed through non-operative care, including remineralization therapy, behavioral changes, and using fluoride-containing products. Remineralization is aimed at stopping the progression of the lesion or, ideally, reversing it. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- History and Physical -- Active or Inactive?
retrieved_docs.content: A carious lesion is considered active when it shows more of these characteristics: it is whitish, matte, has a rough texture (when carefully touched with the tip of a blunt probe), has plaque stagnation, is cavitated, and if there is gingival bleeding. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Diagnostic Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Dental explorers have traditionally diagnosed carious lesions; however, studies have shown that this method causes irreversible harm to the tooth surface and favors the lesion's progression. [3] Dental explorers can disrupt remineralization and transfer cariogenic bacteria to adjacent teeth. Furthermore, using dental explorers to assess occlusal surfaces can result in misdiagnosis - a deep fissure that seems to be a "sticky occlusal surface" could be misdiagnosed as dental caries. Dental explorers should only be used to remove stains and soft plaque from tooth surfaces. [4] Visual examination always comes first when assessing for caries. It requires a dry surface and can be enhanced by magnifying loupes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: changes include senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), general choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), calcific or stone‑type degeneration (تنكس حصوي), partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, partial peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Detachment disorders are captured as serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Scarring, a common morphological outcome of inflammation, trauma or surgery, includes post‑inflammatory choroid scar, post‑surgical choroid scar, post‑traumatic choroid scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, disseminated scar

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is a principal anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular diseases, serving as the locus for hemorrhagic events (نزيف مشيمي – choroidal hemorrhage), tears (التمزق المشيمي – choroidal tear), atrophic changes (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية – senile choroidal atrophy; انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية – secondary choroidal atrophy spread; تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة – choroidal atrophy; تنكس حصوي – calcific degeneration), serous and hemorrhagic detachments (انفصال المشيمية المصلية – serous choroidal detachment; انفصال مشيمية العين – choroidal detachment of the eye; انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي – hemorrhagic choroidal detachment), and sclerotic lesions (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي – partial choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي – total choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula). A wide array of inflammatory and infectious processes also involve the choroidea, including Disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, Focal choroiditis, Generalized disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Juxtapapillary focal choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, and unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis (الالتهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد). Post‑inflammatory and traumatic sequelae frequently produce scarring (Postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, Postinflammatory choroid scar, Postsurgical choroid scar, Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, Posttraumatic choroid scar, ندبة مشيمية شبكية – chorioretinal scar). The choroidea is also linked to بُقَعُ روث. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease assessment, diagnosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: lesion of retina and choroid. The direct procedure site of destruction of lesion of retina and choroid is the retina. Clinical assessment of disease severity, such as disease condition determination, uncontrolled, is interpreted by the general clinical state and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the importance of recognizing uncontrolled secondary diabetes mellitus in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: حصوي (calcific degeneration of choroidal or retinal tissue), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal atrophy) and حثل المشيمية حول الحُليلة، جزئي (partial sclerosis of the choroid surrounding the macula). Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 849
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with destruction of tumor by laser surgery is categorized as a destructive surgical procedure and employs a laser‑based method to achieve tumor ablation.
